food_subgroup: Shellfish stew — mussels and clams in tomato-based broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a combined mussel-and-clam stew with tomato broth. FOODON_03311737 (mussel food product) is used as the closest parent covering the primary shellfish component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Mussels) is the closest defensible parent. No composite shellfish stew code exists in FoodEx2 at the time of processing.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144004 (Mussel substance) is the closest available term. No combined shellfish stew concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One restaurant serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous bowl of mussels and clams in tomato-prawn broth, typically shared or consumed as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Mussels, blue, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15166) and Clams, mixed species, cooked, moist heat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15157), combined with tomato-based broth estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy data for cooked mussels (~45% by weight), cooked clams (~25%), tomato-based sauce (~20%), and prawn broth (~10%), reflecting the described recipe. Energy and macros are cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for shellfish stew analogues. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and seafood stew analogues; Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific shellfish stew. GI of 30 is estimated by analogy with low-GI shellfish and tomato-based seafood stew entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). The dish is protein- and water-dominant with modest carbohydrate from tomato sauce; a low GI of approximately 30 is well-supported by the macronutrient profile. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 20.8) / 100 ≈ 6.2, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 5.2 × 4 = 20.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from prawn broth and shellfish
hypertension.guidance: Each 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 1680 mg sodium, representing 73% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-broth portions or limit consumption to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and a glycemic load of 6.2 per serving, this shellfish stew has a minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from broth versus beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from shellfish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mussels and clams provide cardioprotective omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and low saturated fat, but the prawn broth contributes significant sodium (~1680 mg per serving); individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sodium intake and consider requesting a lighter broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Mussels and White Clams Stew is an Italian-influenced shellfish dish served at Mingle restaurant in Funan, Singapore. The dish features live greenshell mussels and white clams stewed in a pomodoro (tomato) and prawn broth, enriched with cherry tomatoes and finished with katsuobushi (Japanese bonito flakes), which adds a subtle umami depth. A non-spicy version is available on request. The stew has a rich, savoury, mildly acidic tomato base with oceanic sweetness from the fresh shellfish. The texture is tender and juicy, with the mussels and clams opening in the broth during cooking. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~9.8 g per 100 g) and provides excellent levels of vitamin B12 (~8.5 mcg per 100 g), iron (~3.8 mg), selenium, and phosphorus from the shellfish. Fat content is low (~2.1 g per 100 g) with a favourable unsaturated fat profile. The glycemic index is low (approximately 30), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium content from the prawn broth, which may be relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is served as a main course, typically priced at SGD 26 at the source restaurant.
common_user_queries: Is mussels and clams stew healthy?
Is mussel stew good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked mussels and clams with tomato-based broth, using a weighted-component approach. GI is estimated by analogy with shellfish stew entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish composition is well-described.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the USDA SR Legacy shellfish entries used for this estimate and varies significantly by shellfish origin and season. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA SR Legacy shellfish entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as shellfish and tomato-based broth contain no industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fat in shellfish is below detection threshold — confirmed trace/zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains prawn broth and shellfish from a non-certified restaurant; halal certification status of Mingle Funan is not confirmed. 6. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant shellfish stew portions; actual weight may vary. 7. The dish is listed under Italian cuisine tags (Pizza, Pasta) on field survey, which reflects the restaurant's broader menu category rather than the dish itself; the dish is correctly classified as Italian-influenced shellfish stew.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft constructed with USDA SR Legacy weighted-component nutrient estimation for mussels, clams, tomato broth, and prawn broth.

Iteration 2: Addressed q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_coverage failure. The canonical 'food_name' ('Spicy Mussels and White Clams Stew') was added to 'aliases_by_language.english' and subsequently to the flat 'aliases' array to ensure perfect consistency and comprehensiveness. Confirmed trans_fat_g zero justification in notes. Confirmed vitamin_d_mcg and vitamin_e_mg -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Verified GI category 'low' correct for GI=30 (≤55 threshold). Verified ontology match_type values are all within allowed set (exact/closest_parent/none). All hard gates and Q1-Q8 confirmed passing.
